body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: NTS4PTP - Network Time Security for the Precision Time Protocol

Um first of all this is the notewell. Uh, I already mentioned this before the meeting started, but be sure you're signed in via data tracker. Uh, so you can sign in or use the QR code. So uh we strongly recommend the use of a headset and um make sure your audio and video are off unless you're chairing or presenting. So those are the the meeting tips that are provided. Um, and that's the remainder of the agenda. Any questions or any agenda bashing? Um, and at this point, uh, Eric wants to have the floor for a moment. Uh, yes, thank you very much. Uh just a reminder that now is the time in our annual cycle where the NOMCOM has released the list of open positions that need to be filled. Uh I do not intend to run for a fourth term. So if you want to be an internet area director or director of any other area or an IAB member or fulfill any of the other positions posted, uh you should please uh apply. You can nominate yourself. Uh if you would like to find out what it means to be an area director, uh there are at least two things you can do. Uh or three things you can do. Thank you. >> There are a lot of open leadership positions in the IATF. It's a lot of um and we strongly encourage new people uh to step forward um and take advantage of them. All right. Um the next thing is the just a quick status of documents. Um rough time we did a working group last call earlier this year. We had a virtual interim roughly a month month or so ago. Uh and rough time is ready to go to the ISG. The only thing we're holding up on is a shepherd write up. Um if anybody would like experience and would like to try their hand at doing a shepherd write up um feel free to step forward um otherwise uh myself or diet will do that. Uh and then the second one is the NTP over PTP document that's also uh ready to go to the ISG. Uh we I have two uh new RFC's that we have uh we also acknowledged in the virtual interim but I wanted to go ahead and acknowledge them again here. Uh the first one is the NTP interle modes and so in particular thank you to Mirislav for all the work he did on that and then the second one is the enterprise profile um for PTP. I'd like to thank um Doug and Haiko for all the work that they did in getting that one finally through the process and uh wrapping up the work of the Tik Tok working group. For those of you who will recall when we rechartered uh a few years back, we rechartered the NTP working group to be the network time protocols working group. And so it also incorporates any work that the IETF needs to do in the context of u other protocols besides NTP including rough time and uh PTP. So with that the Tik Tok working group has been uh made dormant I guess. Um pardon >> closed. >> It is actually closed. Okay. I was a tiny bit confused by the me message on data tracker when I was looking at it. So >> I think >> okay maybe that was it. So uh in any event all all work at this stage is coming here. Um so that brings us to the next item on the agenda. I was going to ask Haiko to do a 1588 update. Tell us some of the the work ongoing there. destroy my >> Yeah. Okay. Um so, um I just want to uh um as an intro say that I have no official role in the 1588 working group. So, I'm not a leazison uh uh let's say officer or anything like that. I just happen like a few of you to work in both working groups and uh yeah have been asked to to give you a very short overview of of what's going on over there. Um so um uh 1588 uh uh um or the the the e standard decided a while ago that we are um changing the the mode of operation how to how to uh maintain the standard and to to make create new revisions. uh we we do rollup revisions every five years at least that was a plan right so the last uh uh uh published standard was in 2019 we are currently working on the rollup and if you do the math you uh see that okay five years didn't work out well um uh but in in general there are let's say amendments uh being added to this to the last published version of the standard and uh at a certain point again uh more or less five years every five years all these amendments which have been published will be uh combined into a new rollup revision and then the standard is is basically published again um um with with those amendments um built in and so so one of these rollup revisions is basically I think the first one is is um currently under works that is one big uh part of the work of of the 1588 working group there are a number of amendments uh already in the work as well those are not making it into the rollup revision. They are going into the next rollup revision. One notably is um security uh um upgrade or a security let's say um maintenance standard uh called 1588K. So these amendments get letters attached to the to the standard that's going on. There are some others. Um um this being able to disable the announce message is is is another amendment. Some some communities ask for that. Um up until today the announce message cannot be basically uh not used in in in a 1588 um profile for example. There's some other work um uh dealing with symmetry calibration and so on. So that's uh that's going on. One of the bigger things um uh that's going on is um a new version of PTP or a new let's say variant of PTP that is using client server um mode um that has been now designated to be called CSPTP. So client server PTP um this uh is going to be published under IT 1588.1. Uh so these dot standards uh do not uh automatically mean that they are compatible or interoperable with let's say uh the the parent number uh uh in this case CSPDP is using um message the message format so on the wire uh um message format from 1588 but it is using um a a very NTP like approach of basically uh having a stateless client server way of operating. So the mechanics are very similar. Um uh the current state of that work is that there has been a a contribution uh uh brought forward by a couple of companies um meta um um my own company mineberg uh microchip and and and some others Net Time Logic. Uh and uh at the moment there's a vote going on to adopt this contribution as let's say the working draft. So as a starting point uh uh not to have to start from scratch. So so that is uh that is currently uh done. We we did some uh experiments with this during the hackathon here. Um and one of the uh declared goals um is at least from my side I can only speak for myself here is that we are um using NTS um to secure CSPTP and that's uh possible and according to what we what we uh um yeah researched is is is possible because of the let's say similarities in in between CSP and NTP from the protocol mechanics side of things. Um, yeah. So, I think that's probably all. Um, uh, any questions? >> I don't see any hands. >> Okay, very good. Thank you. Um, I've been providing the 1588 update for a while and I decided it was nice to have somebody else talk about it. Um, so at this stage, uh, Tal, did you want to tell us the status of the NTPV5 effort? >> Yeah, sure. Can you see me? Can you hear me? Uh yes I can hear you. Oh and I can see you. Great. >> Okay. So this is a short update about the NTPV5 spec and mostly a summary of the changes uh since the last ITF meeting since ITF 122. Um so basically a few of the sessions have been revised and these are mostly editorial changes. Um so this includes the abstract, the introduction, the basic concepts uh section and also the security considerations section. And these changes may look like uh significant changes but we didn't change the functionality of the protocol. it was mostly adding more descriptions um extending with um these sections were pretty short beforehand and now they're more detailed. So this is one aspect of the document. The uh differences between version five and version 4 were moved to a separate section. So they're still there but they just have their own section now. Uh beyond that there's also a bunch of different uh updates related to different issues. Some of these were uh discussed on the mailing list. uh for example the uh poll interval uh discussion which um as a result of that there was a recent update to the document which now reflects the uh most updated functionality based on these discussions and um one more aspect which we kind of slightly discussed in the interim meeting is related to the uh requirement document. So one of the things we did was kind of a comparison between the requirement document and the NTPv5 spec and we tried to see whether they are aligned uh whether all the requirements on the requirement document have actually been reflected in the NDP5 spec and um there's a list of kind of a mapping between the two which is available on GitHub but the main point is that in most cases they're aligned. We found about five issues which um may need handling. Three of them are pretty easy to fix in the NTPV5 spec and we plan to do that and there were two open issues uh we raised on the mailing list and there was a little bit of discussion about them. Um but I guess the uh discussion specifically about these two open issues is still ongoing. So, we we'll try to close that on the mailing list and uh hopefully address that um in the next uh couple of months. I think that kind of summarizes what we did so far. I think we still have a few action items uh listed that we want um to to do in the next couple of months in the spec including what I just mentioned with the open issues related to requirements and also a few other issues and uh we're also open to feedback and comments about the um revision we did over the last couple of months. Okay. Are there any questions for TAL? That's pretty easy. Um, as a couple reminders, the uh the GitHub repo for the NTPV5 document uh is well the github repo for the whole working group is linked from the data tracker for the working group. Uh and the NTPv5 document is over there. So you can feel free to put in any pull requests over there for small you know for issues or whatever you wish to do. Um so you can see the current state of the document. Um, and also, uh, Tal alluded to this, but at this stage, we've decided, uh, to pause work on the NTPV5 requirements and just focus on getting the spec done and out the door. Uh, and the the key parts of the motivation and those kinds of things um are being reflected back into the NTPV5 spec. All right, any questions? >> Nope. Thank you very much, Tal. Thank you. >> Uh so next on the agenda is the work ongoing NTS for PTP. Um one of the things I wanted to point out before Martin starts uh talking uh because you we have a number of of words flying around we're currently looking at NTS for both PTP and CSPTP. And so this activity right here is the one for PTP. Um, and we're still sussing out what CSP is going CPTP will look like and how NTS can apply to that. Um, Martin. >> Yes. Uh, hello. So, um, yeah, to to be honest, there is no much news to be poured from our side at the moment. Uh the development of NTS4 PTP draft document is currently on hold as some security aspects first need to be uh to be discussed and defined in the IT 1588 security subcommittee. Um at present uh it appears that NTS4 PTP will function solely as a key management system uh while the uh application of the security parameters and keys will be part of the PTP standard. So this is uh possibly the biggest shift uh now and it's not final but it looks like this is the way we are uh we are uh making progress. Um we are currently reviewing a number of aspects such as replay protection and I believe that we are making progress. It's a bit slow. Uh but uh securing PTP is very complex. PTP has a lot of modes uh different clocks, different situations. Therefore, it's not easy to find a proper way to secure PDP and especially we don't want a security solution only for NPS for PDP. We also want a security solution for CSPDP for a GDI based key management system. So therefore we need something uh for all kinds of possible uh security mechanisms or key management systems. Um, Rhina and I will update the draft document for NDS for PDP in the coming weeks, but there will only be minor changes and I think bigger changes will follow as soon we have specific agreements on for example how we perform or how we do the replay protection or how to transmit specific information. uh so keys for example uh or not just keys uh security uh security parameters uh in the PTP packets. So yeah it takes some time um but we are making progress. So um yeah this is the current situation. Any questions on the NTS for PTP? Thank you, Martin. Um, I'd like to remind everybody that these documents are available and we really do need people to be reading them and reviewing them. So, comments are always welcome on the mailing list. Uh, next is uh Oh, excuse me. Oh, sorry. Now I see you in the queue. Is it not on? You can come up here if you'd like. >> I just want to say >> remote. >> Uh just um I think uh I need to be reminded. Am I going to get questions by other area directors when this comes to the telehat about >> we have there's there is the high level IETF liaison effort and we have never established a separate liaison specifically for 1588 and I have been informally acting in that role so they're well aware of what we're doing and they want us to do the NTS pieces and they're doing the PTP pieces. >> Okay. So if that's reflected in the shepherd write up that's probably satisfactory for any ads who might wonder about why we're doing >> right. I think it would be the same kind of text that we put in the shepherd write up for the enterprise profile. >> Yeah. >> Uh we also have an agreement that um >> yes >> we can give document access to the PTP documents for people that are working on PTP related things in the IETF. >> Okay. Thank you. Just dotting eyes and cotting tees. these before they come to bite me. Um, okay. Any other questions? All right. NTS for pools. NTS for NTP pools. >> Yeah. So, uh, there's been a draft now published about half a year ago, I think, maybe a bit more than that. Um, basically the idea is that we want to get more adoption for NTS because it's still not that widely deployed on the open internet. And one of the identified barriers is that it's currently quite hard for users to uh set up NTS because you need to do you need to choose your own servers. Uh which means that you need to look at a list of them figure out which ones are close to you. That that's a hassle barrier. So the idea is that if we can get a pool in place for that uh that could lower the adoption barrier significantly. Uh the current draft that is out there right now is basically some modifications to the key exchange protocol to allow a pool to act as a key exchange server. Um we are currently at the stage that we have an experimental setup for this that is working. If you want uh to have a server added to that pool, please contact us. Um we are open for more pools. um we have a timeline where we can move this to a reasonably production ready uh setup in a relatively short term. So uh the proposal from us right now is that this draft is ready for adoption by the working group if there's interest in that or that we can move to uh publishing this as an independent uh standard uh documenting how our pool works. Um I guess that's going to be the discussion going forward. What what the interest is in that. >> Uh one quick question on this. Um I noticed that we had talked about doing this as an experimental RFC and I see that the intended status is standards track or um because the adoption question is quite different for experimental versus uh standards track. Um I'm not sure which would be the better one um g given what we're going to do with it. Uh so so currently our goal is to run a pool with uh 99% uptime and and everything uh from 2026 onward uh for as long as we can get uh funding completed for that. Uh so we have funding for 2026 for that. Um but I'm not sure if experimental or standard track status would be the best here um given that plan. >> Try it. Try again to see if it works. Is it turned on >> now? It's on. Yes. Oh, there we go. All right. Um I haven't uh uh uh read the draft so I don't know what how extensive the protocol changes are or are are there any protocol changes or is it all operational? >> Uh there are some protocol changes. So there's uh one, two, three, four. I think we define five new records for uh the NTS key exchange. And there's probably going to be a sixth because we're running into some trouble uh that we haven't fixed in the current draft, but that's going to require probably a sixth uh record. >> And does this use the the uh a regular a built-in extension mechanism or do you actually need to update the uh NTS protocol? It it basically uses the the record format that's already there and just adds some defines some new records and some new um interactions with those records. So that might be doable uh in experimental. I think I need to double check. Um that said, if you if you do pursue experimental, then it's customary to have a section that says uh this is the experiment and these are sort of the parameters that we're going to measure and report back uh for you the parameters that will determine success of the experiment and so on. Um not everything not every experiment actually ever gets back around to reporting its its success status and there's no due time due date or anything like that or deadline. But um I I think the only thing that's coming to mind uh that might be concerning is uh having an experimental draft formally update something, but if it's extensions or additions, that's probably okay. I think I'll need to check the contents of the draft. >> Can you repeat that last sentence? I think I'd need to check the contents of the of of the draft to see how uh uh whether or not NT if if NTS is just being is being like actually updated or it's just uh adding some some extensions where an extension system was already defined. Let's >> see what's actually done. >> Yeah. If it's just adding an extension where extensions were already defined uh or supported, then that's what extension mechanisms are for. So it's kind of like not an updates. Uh >> it it it's a bit complicated. It does uh also modify some of the semantics once these uh extensions are present in the sense that some of the musts in uh the MTSRC are basically we're saying well if you send this extension field you don't actually have to do those. >> I see. So this might this it's probably better if it's PS then >> or standard track. >> Standard track. >> Yeah. >> Okay. So >> I I guess the the question I had versus for experimental was the conversation we had on this in Dublin um was this is one approach to doing pools and there could be others um and we haven't actually done a call to see are there other approaches um >> well we know of a few other approaches um >> and that that was my concern about standardizing an approach that's why I thought we were doing experiment that's why I thought we originally agreed to experimental Um >> would uh would um the post status for this actually preclude other people from doing other approaches? >> Uh no. >> And furthermore, if you don't implement the spec, uh you won't notice it's there. So as long as you don't touch these extension fields, uh you don't have to worry about the spec existing. >> Okay. I mean I I agree experimental would be nice uh if it's because it could be an experiment but if there's >> and I guess technically we can we we have been known to change the status of something after we've started working on it but >> well >> yeah that that's all that's all fixable >> up up until the point yeah >> right but so just to clarify David you're saying that um you can implement this spec and people that are doing straight NTS as currently documented won't be impacted. >> Yes. >> Okay. >> All right. Um is there any questions or comments on this uh document? >> All right. Um so the next call next step for this would be um whether we want to do a call for adoption or not on this. Um, I think we need a little bit more discussion on the mailing list. Um, so if I could ask you to post to the mailing list a reminder that this document has been updated and that we're going to ask for a call for adoption and we will pursue that call for adoption in the time frame leading up to our next virtual interim. >> Okay. >> Is that acceptable? >> Uh, should be. >> All right. and um you know any updates you have on how the pool is working or how the experiment is going would be welcome to the mailing list or here. >> Well, so far it seems to be working fine, but we haven't really started putting load on it. So, okay, >> we'll see over the next few weeks. >> Okay. Um this is a a definitely a good step forward because it is one of the major barriers to getting NTS more widely deployed. So, thank you for that. Any questions? Okay. Uh the next um item is an update on we did have a small hackathon uh table this time and uh David is also going to report out on all of the work that was done there. >> Yeah. So uh update on the hackathon. There was some work on NTS pools. Uh we have a pool running. We have servers in that pool. We've added a few servers during the hackathon from people who uh spent some time during the hackathon trying to set one up. Experience from that was that it was reasonably straightforward to set up once you have a server running that is actually reachable from the internet. That honestly was for most people the largest barrier. Um we've done some work on NTPv5 in that uh the two major implementations Crony and uh NPDRS now uh support the same draft version. Again, um we did not do much interoperability testing. That's still on the to-do list for some point. Um I don't expect many troubles there, but um we'll see once we actually do that testing. Um and finally there's been some work on uh CSP uh in combination with NTS uh which is more in the 1588 realm. Uh we have a few uh we've made a few CSP implementations and started experimenting a bit with adding NTS on top of those. Um but we're not anything concrete there yet. And I think that covers everything unless I'm missing something else. All right. For anybody interested in any of the uh code repos that were associated with the work that was done this week, um they're all linked from the uh hackathon wiki. So if you Google IETF-123 uh hackathon and find the wiki associated with the hackathon, it'll have a description of all the various projects pieces with links to documentation um and give you an idea of what what was going on. So thank you to everybody who participated in that. Um and maybe it's because it's first thing in the morning, but we seem to be ripping right through all of this. Uh so we have come to any other business. Is there any other business? Anybody have any last minute questions? Okay. We will plan a virtual interim in the September time frame and our next uh physical so we'll have a virtual meeting in September and the next uh NTP at an IATF meeting will be in Montreal in November. And with that, I give you all an extra hour and a half in your day. Let's do this. [Music] Yes. So there's They're only
